title: Intraosseous access in the resuscitation of patients with trauma: the good, the bad, the future
one_line_summary: IO access is a rapid, high-success temporary bridge in trauma resuscitation, but has flow limitations, hemolysis risk, and high dislodgement rates; definitive access and USG-PIV are crucial alternatives.
key_pearls: Establish IO access immediately when PIV attempts fail in a shocked trauma patient, but initiate parallel attempts for definitive access (large-bore PIV, USG-PIV, or central venous sheath) and do not maintain IO access beyond acute resuscitation.
For active hemorrhagic shock, place two IO catheters (ideally humeral or sternal for superior flow) to achieve a combined flow rate capable of matching blood loss >= 150 mL/min.
Always use a 45 mm needle for humeral IO in adults to prevent extravasation and dislodgement, and strictly secure the arm to prevent rotation.
For conscious patients, slowly infuse lidocaine 1-2% at 0.5 mg/kg through the IO hub prior to flushing or running fluids to mitigate severe marrow expansion pain.
Do not infuse hypertonic saline through an IO line due to the risk of bone marrow and soft tissue necrosis.

sections.heading: Types and Sites of IO Access
sections.content: Access Modalities include Manual (hand-inserted), Mechanical (spring-loaded), and Power Drill (battery-operated, e.g., EZ-IO) systems. Anatomical Sites & Clinical Nuances vary: Tibial (1-2 cm inferior/medial to tibial tuberosity) is most common but has lowest flow rates. Humeral (humeral head) requires a longer 45 mm needle and is vulnerable to dislodgement. Sternal (middle manubrium sternum) offers fastest flow rates, common in military, but contraindicated in pediatrics due to mediastinal/cardiac penetration risk. Pharmacokinetics show tibial IO absorption comparable to large-bore PIV, while humeral and sternal mimic central venous access, delivering drugs rapidly to the right atrium.

sections.heading: Training, Speed, and Success Rates
sections.content: IO access is easy to train, with novice users achieving high success rates rapidly. Levitan et al. reported 97.3% success after a 5-minute video and cadaveric instruction. Anderson et al. showed 87% prehospital success after a 1-hour standardized training. Insertion Speed & Success studies demonstrate IO's advantage: Chreiman et al. (Video Review) found IO access equally fast as PIV but with significantly higher success rates (95% IO vs. 42% PIV vs. 46% CVC) in patients in extremis. Dumas et al. (EAST Multicenter Study) evaluated 1,410 attempts in 581 hypotensive trauma patients, reporting 93% IO success (vs. 67% PIV and 59% CVC). Time to resuscitation initiation was faster in the IO group (5.8 minutes) compared to the PIV group (6.7 minutes).

sections.heading: Analgesia Considerations
sections.content: The primary source of severe pain during IO infusion is not needle insertion but the expansion of the intraosseous marrow space. If time permits, infiltrate the periosteum with local anesthetic prior to insertion. During active resuscitation, experts recommend administering lidocaine 1% to 2% at a dose of 0.5 mg/kg through the IO needle prior to initiating fluid or medication infusions.

sections.heading: Flow Rate Dynamics & Limitations
sections.content: Flow is dictated by the porous structure of cancellous bone, which varies by age, gender, and bone density. Gravity Flow Rates (mL/min, Time to infuse 1 L in min): 14G IV: 250 mL/min (4 min); 16G IV: 150 mL/min (7 min); 7.5 French Introducer Sheath: 130 mL/min (8 min); 18G IV: 100 mL/min (10 min); 15G Humeral IO: 80 mL/min (13 min); 15G Tibial IO: 70 mL/min (15 min); 20G IV: 60 mL/min (17 min). With pressure bags, tibial flow rates can reach 165 mL/min (dropping to 73 mL/min without pressure), and humeral flow rates reach 153 mL/min with pressure (84 mL/min without). The Shock State Paradox: In hemorrhagic shock/hypovolemia, vasoconstriction and marrow collapse decrease IO infusion rates by approximately 32%. Since major hemorrhage is defined as blood loss >= 150 mL/min, a single IO catheter is hemodynamically insufficient to match active blood loss in severe trauma.

sections.heading: Red Cell Hemolysis & Complications
sections.content: Hemolysis Risk: High pressure (e.g., 300 mm Hg) needed for high flow rates generates severe shearing forces that can destroy red blood cells, reducing oxygen-carrying capacity and potentially causing rhabdomyolysis. A human study of sternal IO whole blood transfusion (Bjerkvig et al.) documented significant increases in lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) and decreases in hemoglobin post-infusion. Complications in Adults: The overall complication rate is 4.6% after successful insertion. These include Extravasation/Displacement (2.8%, ranging 1-22% depending on operator experience, potentially leading to compartment syndrome), Device Malfunction (1.8%), and Needle Dislodgement (highly prevalent in humeral access at 20% vs. 9% in tibial, often due to patient movement and inadequate 45 mm needle length). Rare complications include Tissue Necrosis (0.02%) and Infection/Osteomyelitis (0.01%). Hypertonic saline is contraindicated in IO lines due to animal data showing bone and soft tissue necrosis.

sections.heading: Clinical Outcomes & The Out-of-Hospital Cardiac Arrest (OHCA) Signal
sections.content: While IO access is faster, large-scale clinical trials in OHCA show a concerning signal. Mody et al. (19,731 patients) demonstrated that IO access was associated with worse survival to discharge (4.6% vs. 5.7%, p=0.01), lower sustained ROSC (17.9% vs. 23.5%, p<0.001), and poorer favorable neurological outcomes (2.8% vs. 4.2%, p<0.001) compared to IV access. Daya et al. confirmed drug efficacy (amiodarone/lidocaine) was significantly better via the IV route than the IO route.

sections.heading: Ultrasound-Guided PIV (USG-PIV) as an Alternative
sections.content: USG-PIV significantly increases success rates in patients with difficult vascular access (collapsing veins, obesity, IV drug abuse). In hypotensive animal models (BP < 90 mm Hg), blind PIV success was 17% (1/6) while USG-PIV success was 89% (8/9) with a median time of 65 seconds. Systematic reviews confirm USG-PIV reduces puncture attempts, shortens procedure times, and improves patient satisfaction.

strengths_limitations: Strengths of IO access include rapid insertion (<1 min), high first-pass success rates (93-95%) in unstable patients, and minimal training. Limitations are insufficient flow for massive hemorrhage (tibial IO gravity flow 70 mL/min), a 32% drop in flow in hypovolemic shock, high red cell hemolysis risk under pressure, high dislodgement rates (20% humeral), and no proven survival or neurological outcome benefits.
